extension.valueMarkdown:
In 2022, during a depressive episode and work stress, Sarah developed problematic alcohol use that escalated to alcohol use disorder (moderate severity). She self-referred to an outpatient addiction medicine program in Portland in early 2023. She completed a 12-week intensive outpatient program (IOP), began attending AA meetings, and was prescribed naltrexone 50mg daily. She achieved sobriety in April 2023 and has maintained it.

In mid-2024, Sarah moved to Seattle. She established with a new psychiatrist for bipolar management and a new PCP for general care. Her alcohol use disorder is in sustained remission (>12 months). She continues lamotrigine, quetiapine, and naltrexone.

Sarah uses a patient-facing app to access her health records across her Portland and Seattle providers. The behavioral health and substance use disorder data is subject to heightened sensitivity protections (42 CFR Part 2 for SUD records).

note.text: Depressive phase worsening at quarterly follow-up (2022-11-14). PHQ-9 increased from 7 to 14 (moderate depression) over three months. Possible contributing factors include financial stressors, possible increased alcohol use, and seasonal component. Lamotrigine level therapeutic at 4.8 mcg/mL. Continued lamotrigine 200 mg daily and quetiapine 50 mg nightly. Follow-up interval shortened to 6 weeks.

coding.display: Major depressive disorder, single episode, moderate

note.text: Reclassified as the depressive component of bipolar II disorder; resolved as an independent diagnosis. Original diagnosis made by PCP; upon comprehensive psychiatric evaluation, mood episodes are better accounted for by bipolar II disorder.

coding.display: Insomnia disorder related to another mental disorder (disorder)

note.text: Previously active. Sleep normalized at 7-8 hours nightly on current lamotrigine regimen. Resolved as of this encounter.
GAD-7 score 10 (moderate anxiety). Likely situational in the context of COVID-19 pandemic stressors and financial uncertainty.

Significant sleep disruption (3-4 hours per night despite exhaustion), contributing to and exacerbated by bipolar II depressive episode. Quetiapine 50 mg at bedtime initiated. Sleep hygiene counseling provided.

coding.display: Alcohol intake above recommended sensible limits (finding)

note.text: Patient reports nightly wine use 'to relax' but minimizes quantity. Given bipolar disorder, any regular alcohol use risks mood destabilization and medication interactions. Patient not forthcoming about the full extent of drinking. Will continue to monitor closely.

Triglycerides elevated to 178 mg/dL, up from 148 mg/dL at prior check. Likely quetiapine-related metabolic effect, though increased alcohol intake could also be contributing. Will continue metabolic monitoring.

note.text: Self-reported by patient during telephone call. Score of 5 consistent with mild depressive symptoms, improved from 14 (moderate) at last documented assessment.

dosageInstruction.text: 25 mg daily x 2 weeks, then 50 mg daily x 2 weeks, then 100 mg daily x 2 weeks, then 150 mg daily x 2 weeks, then 200 mg daily (target dose)
dosageInstruction.patientInstruction: Take 25 mg by mouth daily for 2 weeks, then increase as directed by your doctor to a target dose of 200 mg daily. Discontinue and seek immediate medical attention if any rash develops.

note.text: Slow titration to minimize Stevens-Johnson syndrome risk: 25 mg x 2 weeks → 50 mg x 2 weeks → 100 mg x 2 weeks → 150 mg x 2 weeks → 200 mg daily target. Patient counseled regarding rash risk and instructed to discontinue and seek immediate medical attention if rash develops.

reasonCode.text: Identified as precipitant of medication-induced hypomania in context of bipolar II disorder; antidepressant monotherapy contraindicated in bipolar disorder

dosageInstruction.text: Taper: 100 mg daily to 50 mg daily x 2 weeks, then 25 mg daily x 2 weeks, then discontinue
dosageInstruction.patientInstruction: Taper: Take 50 mg daily for 2 weeks, then 25 mg daily for 2 weeks, then stop.

note.text: Taper sertraline over 4 weeks: 100 mg → 50 mg daily × 2 weeks → 25 mg daily × 2 weeks → discontinue. Sertraline identified as precipitant of medication-induced hypomanic episode. Taper completed successfully by 2019-10-07 without significant withdrawal symptoms.

reasonCode.text: Insomnia and mood stabilization as adjunct to lamotrigine for bipolar II depressive episode

dosageInstruction.text: Take 50 mg by mouth at bedtime
dosageInstruction.patientInstruction: Take 50 mg by mouth at bedtime.

note.text: Initiated for insomnia and mood stabilization as adjunct to lamotrigine 200 mg daily for bipolar II depressive episode. Discussed expected sedation and potential metabolic side effects (weight gain, glucose/lipid changes). Baseline metabolic labs ordered.

dosageInstruction.text: Take 200 mg by mouth once daily
dosageInstruction.patientInstruction: Take 200 mg by mouth once daily.

note.text: 90-day bridge prescription provided ahead of patient's relocation from Portland to Seattle. Intended to ensure no medication gap during care transition to a new psychiatrist.

dosageInstruction.text: Take 50 mg by mouth at bedtime
dosageInstruction.patientInstruction: Take 50 mg by mouth at bedtime.

resource.conclusion: Metabolic monitoring for quetiapine safety surveillance. Glucose 96 mg/dL (normal). Triglycerides 178 mg/dL (elevated, up from 148 mg/dL). Triglyceride elevation likely quetiapine-related with possible alcohol contribution.
